hazard.summary: Ambiguous in-band rate-limit signals without an explicit retry time were escalated into account-level runtime blocks using availability reset time, removing otherwise healthy accounts from routing and producing immediate 503 no-routable-account failures.
signals: in-band usage/rate-limit signal without parseable retryAt
accounts with remaining quota blocked for several hours
aggregate gateway unavailable: no routable OpenAI account
all routing candidates removed despite accounts showing available quota
maintainer fix restricted reset-time fallback to quotaExhausted cases

constraints: Ambiguous rate-limit signals must not escalate to account-level blocking
Reset-time fallback must only be used when quota exhaustion is explicitly confirmed
Fallback logic must preserve routing eligibility for accounts that still have usable quota
Router decisions must distinguish uncertain in-band pressure from definitive quota exhaustion
Runtime block scope must match the certainty and scope of the underlying signal
verification: Simulate an in-band rate-limit signal with no parseable retryAt on an account that still has quota
Verify the account is not blocked until availabilityResetAt
Verify ambiguous signals fall back to a bounded default cooldown
Verify confirmed quotaExhausted accounts still use reset-time semantics
Verify routing candidates are not emptied when accounts remain usable
Verify no immediate 503 no-routable-account response occurs solely from ambiguous in-band signals
notes: This is a signal-to-scope mismatch. The runtime treated an ambiguous in-band rate or usage signal as if it definitively proved account-level quota exhaustion, then used availabilityResetAt to block accounts for hours. The maintainer fix restricted reset-time fallback to confirmed quotaExhausted cases and preserved shorter fallback behavior for ambiguous non-exhausted signals. The reusable lesson is that fallback logic must not promote uncertain signals into broad enforcement actions.
